original_hypothesis.title: FeeSplitter userTokens Array Accumulates Unbounded Duplicates Causing DoS on Batch Claims
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange pushes token to userTokens[account] every time it's called with balance > 0, without checking for duplicates. Since onBalanceChange is called on every buy (and can be called by anyone due to broken onlyManager), the array grows unboundedly with duplicate entries. getUserTokensAndClaimable and batchClaiming iterate this array, eventually hitting gas limits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Unbounded array growth is a known DoS vector. Combined with broken access control, anyone can bloat any user's array by calling onBalanceChange repeatedly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (balanceOf(token, account) > 0) userTokens[account].push(token); — no duplicate check, called on every buy and externally callable

preconditions: User buys tokens multiple times (normal usage) or attacker calls onBalanceChange repeatedly (broken access control)
User later tries to use batchClaiming or getUserTokensAndClaimable

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Array push without duplicate check in frequently-called function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userTokens grows O(n) with number of buys, not O(unique tokens)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No duplicate check, no array size limit

analysis_questions: How many duplicates can be added before batchClaiming exceeds block gas limit?
Does batchClaiming double-count fees from duplicates?
Can individual claimFees still work when batchClaiming is DoS'd?
validation_requirements: Call onBalanceChange repeatedly, measure userTokens growth
Attempt batchClaiming with large array, verify gas limit hit
Check if duplicate entries cause double fee claims

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls onBalanceChange(token, victim) thousands of times (free due to broken access control). Victim's userTokens array grows to thousands of entries. Victim's batchClaiming reverts with out-of-gas. Individual claimFees still works but batch functionality is permanently broken.
prerequisites: Broken onlyManager (TECH-H001) for weaponized version; normal usage for organic growth

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of batch claiming functionality; individual claims still possible but more expensive
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user targeted by attacker or heavy traders

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for userTokens array growth and gas limits

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate entries needed to exceed block gas limit
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Gas costs for repeated onBalanceChange calls
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multiple transactions to bloat, then triggered on victim's batch claim
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large number of onBalanceChange calls for same user detectable

validation_result.impact_summary: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange pushes duplicates to userTokens[account] on every call with balance > 0, with no duplicate check. Combined with broken onlyManager, anyone can bloat any user's array, causing DoS on getUserTokensAndClaimable. batchClaiming itself is NOT affected since it uses a caller-supplied tokenList parameter.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The code defect is confirmed: FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange at line 92 unconditionally pushes token to userTokens[account] whenever balanceOf(token, account) > 0, with no duplicate check. This means every buy transaction (which calls onBalanceChange via _transferFees) adds a duplicate entry. Furthermore, because Security.sol's onlyManager modifier is a no-op (evaluates `managers[msg.sender] == true;` without require/revert), anyone can call onBalanceChange directly for any user, weaponizing the array growth at minimal gas cost. The primary on-chain impact is on getUserTokensAndClaimable, which iterates the full userTokens array creating a memory array of UserClaimData structs. However, batchClaiming takes an external `address[] calldata tokenList` parameter—it does NOT iterate userTokens—so the specific claim about batchClaiming DoS is inaccurate. The array also cannot be pruned as there is no removal function. The code defect (missing duplicate check on array push) is real regardless of the precise impact characterization.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Line 92 of FeeSplitter.sol clearly shows `userTokens[account].push(token)` with no prior check for whether token already exists in the array. Compare this with Curves.sol's `_addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject` which DOES have a duplicate check (iterating to find existing entries before pushing). The FeeSplitter lacks this safeguard entirely. Security.sol lines 8-9 confirm the onlyManager modifier is a no-op: `managers[msg.sender] == true;` is a bare expression statement with no require.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Pushes token to userTokens[account] on every invocation where balance > 0, with no check for existing entries. Called on every buy via _transferFees, and also directly callable by anyone due to broken onlyManager modifier. Array grows O(n) with number of calls, not O(unique tokens).
Modifier evaluates a boolean expression as a statement without require(), making it a no-op. This allows anyone to call onBalanceChange and weaponize the array bloat.

Iterates the full userTokens array which contains duplicates, causing excessive gas consumption. Each iteration also calls getClaimableFees which makes an external call to curves.curvesTokenBalance, compounding the gas cost.

calling_sequence: 1. Normal path: User calls buyCurvesToken (Curves.sol:199) → _buyCurvesToken (Curves.sol:225) → _transferFees (Curves.sol:204) → feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange(curvesTokenSubject, msg.sender) at Curves.sol:222
2. Weaponized path: Attacker directly calls onBalanceChange(token, victim) — onlyManager modifier is no-op (Security.sol:11-14)
3. onBalanceChange (FeeSplitter.sol:89-93): sets userFeeOffset, then checks balanceOf > 0, pushes token to userTokens[account]
4. No duplicate check exists — contrast with Curves._addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject (Curves.sol:264-271) which does check
5. Victim calls getUserTokensAndClaimable → iterates bloated array → gas limit exceeded for on-chain calls or very expensive eth_call
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability flows from two vectors: (1) organic growth through repeated buys of the same token, and (2) weaponized growth through direct calls to onBalanceChange. In both cases, FeeSplitter.sol:92 (`userTokens[account].push(token)`) appends without deduplication. The impact materializes when getUserTokensAndClaimable (FeeSplitter.sol:55-63) iterates the bloated array. Note: batchClaiming (FeeSplitter.sol:96-108) takes `address[] calldata tokenList` as a parameter and does NOT read from userTokens, so it is NOT directly affected by this DoS vector, contrary to the hypothesis claim.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that contradict the findings. The defect is in the contract code itself.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is partially valid. An attacker CAN call onBalanceChange(token, victim) thousands of times due to the broken onlyManager modifier, bloating victim's userTokens array with duplicates. getUserTokensAndClaimable WILL become DoS'd for on-chain calls. However, batchClaiming is NOT DoS'd because it uses a caller-supplied tokenList parameter, not userTokens. Individual claimFees also still works. The financial impact is limited: no double-counting of fees occurs even with duplicates (updateFeeCredit resets offset on first pass, second pass computes 0 owed). The primary impact is degraded UX via broken view function and permanently bloated storage.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity confirmed. The code defect is real: unbounded duplicate array growth with no pruning mechanism. With broken access control, any user's userTokens can be bloated by anyone at gas-only cost. getUserTokensAndClaimable becomes unusable on-chain. However, the financial impact is limited because: (1) batchClaiming uses caller-supplied list, not userTokens, (2) claimFees works per-token without iterating, (3) no fee double-counting occurs from duplicates. Storage bloat is permanent and irrecoverable.
recommended_mitigations: Add duplicate check in onBalanceChange before pushing: iterate userTokens[account] to check if token already exists (similar to _addOwnedCurvesTokenSubject in Curves.sol)
Alternatively, use a mapping(address => mapping(address => bool)) to track which tokens are already in a user's array
Fix the onlyManager modifier to use require(): require(managers[msg.sender] == true, 'Not manager');
Consider adding an array size limit as a safety cap
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves+FeeSplitter+Security with broken modifiers. 2. Have user buy token once (creates initial userTokens entry). 3. Call onBalanceChange(token, user) from any address 10,000 times. 4. Verify userTokens[user].length == 10,001. 5. Call getUserTokensAndClaimable(user) and measure gas. 6. Verify batchClaiming still works with a manually-supplied single-entry tokenList. 7. Verify no fee double-counting by checking claimable amount equals expected single-token claimable.
uncertainties: The hypothesis claims batchClaiming is DoS'd, but batchClaiming uses a caller-supplied tokenList, not userTokens — the DoS is on getUserTokensAndClaimable, not batchClaiming
View functions can be called off-chain with higher gas limits by nodes, so the practical DoS threshold depends on node configuration
The gas cost of each onBalanceChange call limits how quickly the array can be bloated (attacker pays gas for each call)
additional_testing_needed: Measure exact gas cost of getUserTokensAndClaimable with 1000, 10000, 100000 entries to find practical DoS threshold
Verify batchClaiming works correctly with manually-supplied tokenList regardless of userTokens size
Test whether duplicate entries in a user-supplied tokenList to batchClaiming cause any fee double-counting (preliminary analysis suggests no)
